A [general election](/wiki/General_elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "General elections in the United Kingdom") was held in the United Kingdom on 4 July 2024 to elect all 650 members of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). The opposition [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), led by [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer"), won a [landslide victory](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory") over the governing [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") under Prime Minister [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak"), ending 14 years of Conservative government.[[3]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk22-8)

Labour secured 411 seats[[f]](#cite_note-9)[[b]](#cite_note-Speaker-4) and a 174-seat majority, the third-best showing in the party's history and its best since [2001](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election"). The party's vote share was 33.7%, the lowest of any governing party on record, making this the [least proportional](#Proportionality_concerns) general election in British history.[[4]](#cite_note-10) They became the largest party in England, Scotland and Wales. The Conservatives suffered their worst-ever defeat, winning 121 seats with 23.7% of the vote and losing 251 seats, including those of the former prime minister [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") and 12 [Cabinet ministers](/wiki/Sunak_ministry "Sunak ministry").[[5]](#cite_note-11)

Smaller parties saw record support, with 42.6% of the total vote. The [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), led by [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey"), became the third-largest party with 72 seats, their best result ever and better than any [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") result [since 1923](/wiki/1923_United_Kingdom_general_election "1923 United Kingdom general election"). [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"), led by [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), won five seats and 14.3% of the vote, the third-highest vote share, and the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") won four seats. For both parties this was their best parliamentary result to date.

In Scotland the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") dropped from 48 to 9 seats, losing its status as Scotland's largest party.[[6]](#cite_note-12) In Wales, [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") won four seats. In Northern Ireland, which has a [distinct set of political parties](/wiki/Politics_of_Northern_Ireland#Political_parties "Politics of Northern Ireland"),[[7]](#cite_note-13) [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") retained seven seats; the first election in which an [Irish nationalist](/wiki/Irish_nationalist "Irish nationalist") party won the most seats in Northern Ireland. The [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") dropped from eight to five seats.

Campaign issues included the [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom"), [healthcare](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"), [housing](/wiki/Housing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Housing in the United Kingdom"), [energy](/wiki/Energy_in_the_United_Kingdom "Energy in the United Kingdom") and [immigration](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom"). There was relatively little discussion of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), which was a major issue during the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election#Policy_positions "2019 United Kingdom general election"). This was the first general election under the [Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022](/wiki/Dissolution_and_Calling_of_Parliament_Act_2022 "Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022"), the first with [photo identification](/wiki/Photo_identification "Photo identification") required to vote in Great Britain,[[g]](#cite_note-NI-14) and the first contested using the new constituency boundaries implemented following the [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies").[[8]](#cite_note-15)

The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") under [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") won a large majority at the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") and the new government passed the [Brexit withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement").[[9]](#cite_note-16)[[10]](#cite_note-17) The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") saw the government institute [extensive public health restrictions](/wiki/United_Kingdom_responses_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "United Kingdom responses to the COVID-19 pandemic"), including limitations on social interaction, that Johnson and some of his staff were later found to have broken. The resulting [political scandal](/wiki/Political_scandal "Political scandal") ([Partygate](/wiki/Partygate "Partygate")), one of many [controversies](/wiki/Category:Boris_Johnson_controversies "Category:Boris Johnson controversies") during [Johnson's premiership](/wiki/Johnson%27s_premiership "Johnson's premiership"), saw the Conservatives lose their poll lead.[[11]](#cite_note-James-2023-18)[[12]](#cite_note-RTTimes-2022-19) These scandals culminated in the [resignation of over 50 ministers from the Government](/wiki/July_2022_United_Kingdom_government_crisis "July 2022 United Kingdom government crisis") following [allegations of sexual misconduct against a government minister](/wiki/Chris_Pincher_scandal "Chris Pincher scandal"). Johnson announced his resignation in July 2022, to become effective from the election of a successor.[[13]](#cite_note-20) He resigned as an MP the following year,[[14]](#cite_note-21) after [an investigation](/wiki/Commons_Privileges_Committee_investigation_into_Boris_Johnson "Commons Privileges Committee investigation into Boris Johnson") unanimously found that he had lied to Parliament.[[15]](#cite_note-22)

[Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") won the resultant [leadership election](/wiki/July%E2%80%93September_2022_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "July–September 2022 Conservative Party leadership election") and succeeded Johnson.[[16]](#cite_note-23)[[17]](#cite_note-24) Truss announced [large-scale tax cuts and borrowing in a mini-budget](/wiki/September_2022_United_Kingdom_mini-budget "September 2022 United Kingdom mini-budget") on 23 September, although many of its measures were reversed following financial instability.[[18]](#cite_note-25) She [resigned](/wiki/October_2022_United_Kingdom_government_crisis "October 2022 United Kingdom government crisis") in October, making her the [shortest-serving prime minister in British history](/wiki/Shortest-serving_prime_minister_in_British_history "Shortest-serving prime minister in British history"),[[19]](#cite_note-26) and was [succeeded unopposed](/wiki/October_2022_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "October 2022 Conservative Party leadership election") by [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak").[[20]](#cite_note-27)

During [his premiership](/wiki/Premiership_of_Rishi_Sunak "Premiership of Rishi Sunak"), Sunak was credited with improving the economy and stabilising national politics following the premierships of his predecessors,[[21]](#cite_note-28) although many of his pledges and policy announcements ultimately went unfulfilled.[[22]](#cite_note-29)[[23]](#cite_note-30) He did not avert further unpopularity for the Conservatives who, by the time of Sunak's election, had been in government for 12 years. Public opinion in favour of a change in government was reflected in the Conservatives' poor performance at the [2022](/wiki/2022_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2022 United Kingdom local elections"), [2023](/wiki/2023_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2023 United Kingdom local elections") and [2024 local elections](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2024 United Kingdom local elections").[[24]](#cite_note-econpredict0424-31)

### Political background of other parties before the election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political background of other parties before the election")]

[Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") won the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")'s [2020 leadership election](/wiki/2020_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2020 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), succeeding [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn").[[25]](#cite_note-32) Under his leadership, Starmer repositioned the party away from the [left](/wiki/Labour_left "Labour left") and toward the [political centre](/wiki/Political_centre "Political centre"), and emphasised the elimination of [antisemitism within the party](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_British_Labour_Party "Antisemitism in the British Labour Party").[[26]](#cite_note-33)[[27]](#cite_note-34) Starmer's leading his party rightward in order to improve its electability has been widely compared to [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair")'s development of [New Labour](/wiki/New_Labour "New Labour") in the 1990s. The political turmoil from the Conservative scandals and government crises led to Labour having a significant lead in polling over the Conservatives, often by very wide margins, since late 2021, coinciding with the start of the Partygate scandal.[[11]](#cite_note-James-2023-18)[[12]](#cite_note-RTTimes-2022-19) Labour made gains in local elections: in the 2023 local elections, Labour gained more than 500 councillors and 22 councils, becoming the largest party in local government for the first time since 2002.[[28]](#cite_note-35)

[Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey"), who previously served in the [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition"), won the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")' [2020 leadership election](/wiki/2020_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2020 Liberal Democrats leadership election"), succeeding [Jo Swinson](/wiki/Jo_Swinson "Jo Swinson"), who lost her seat in the previous general election.[[29]](#cite_note-36) Davey prioritised defeating the Conservatives and ruled out working with them following the election.[[30]](#cite_note-37) The Liberal Democrats made gains in local elections: in the 2024 local elections, the Liberal Democrats finished second for the first time in a local election cycle since 2009.[[31]](#cite_note-38)

Like the Conservatives, the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) suffered political turmoil and saw a decrease in their popularity in opinion polling, with multiple party leaders and First Ministers ([Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon"), [Humza Yousaf](/wiki/Humza_Yousaf "Humza Yousaf") and [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney")) and the [Operation Branchform](/wiki/Operation_Branchform "Operation Branchform") police investigation. Sturgeon claimed [occupational burnout](/wiki/Occupational_burnout "Occupational burnout") was the reason for her resignation,[[32]](#cite_note-39) while Yousaf resigned amid [a government crisis](/wiki/2024_Scottish_government_crisis "2024 Scottish government crisis") following his termination of [a power-sharing agreement](/wiki/Bute_House_Agreement "Bute House Agreement") with the [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens").[[33]](#cite_note-40) When Swinney [assumed the leadership](/wiki/Premiership_of_John_Swinney "Premiership of John Swinney") after being elected unopposed to succeed Yousaf, the SNP had been in government for 17 years.[[34]](#cite_note-41)

[Carla Denyer](/wiki/Carla_Denyer "Carla Denyer") and [Adrian Ramsay](/wiki/Adrian_Ramsay "Adrian Ramsay") took over leadership of the [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") from [Siân Berry](/wiki/Si%C3%A2n_Berry "Siân Berry") and [Jonathan Bartley](/wiki/Jonathan_Bartley "Jonathan Bartley") in 2021. [Rhun ap Iorwerth](/wiki/Rhun_ap_Iorwerth "Rhun ap Iorwerth") took over leadership of [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru"). The Brexit Party rebranded as [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"), and was initially led by [Richard Tice](/wiki/Richard_Tice "Richard Tice") in the years preceding the election before [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") resumed leadership during the election campaign.[[35]](#cite_note-42) [Edwin Poots](/wiki/Edwin_Poots "Edwin Poots") took over as the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") leader in May 2021 but lasted only 20 days. He was replaced by [Jeffrey Donaldson](/wiki/Jeffrey_Donaldson "Jeffrey Donaldson"), who resigned in March 2024 after being arrested on charges relating to historical sex offences. He appeared in court on 3 July, the day before polling day, to face additional sex offence charges.[[36]](#cite_note-43)[[37]](#cite_note-44) [Gavin Robinson](/wiki/Gavin_Robinson "Gavin Robinson") initially took over as interim leader,[[38]](#cite_note-45) and then became the permanent leader in May.[[39]](#cite_note-46)

New political parties who made their campaign debuts in this election included the [Alba Party](/wiki/Alba_Party "Alba Party"), led by former Scottish First Minister [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond"), and the [Workers Party of Britain](/wiki/Workers_Party_of_Britain "Workers Party of Britain"), led by anti-war activist [George Galloway](/wiki/George_Galloway "George Galloway"), who won the [2024 Rochdale by-election](/wiki/2024_Rochdale_by-election "2024 Rochdale by-election") in a [political upset](/wiki/Political_upset "Political upset") three months before the election was called – advertising himself as a protest candidate against Labour's stance on the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and appealing to the constituency's sizeable Muslim population.[[40]](#cite_note-47)

## Date of the election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Date of the election")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Dissolution_of_Parliament_2024_Royal_Proclamation_%2815%29.jpg/250px-Dissolution_of_Parliament_2024_Royal_Proclamation_%2815%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dissolution_of_Parliament_2024_Royal_Proclamation_(15).jpg)

Reading of the [dissolution proclamation](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Dissolution of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") on the steps of the [Royal Exchange](/wiki/Royal_Exchange,_London "Royal Exchange, London")

Originally, the next election was scheduled to take place on 2 May 2024 under the [Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011](/wiki/Fixed-term_Parliaments_Act_2011 "Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011").[[o]](#cite_note-67) At the 2019 general election, in which the Conservatives won a majority of 80 seats, the party's manifesto contained a commitment to repeal the Fixed-term Parliaments Act.[[53]](#cite_note-68) In December 2020, the government duly published a draft Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 (Repeal) Bill, later retitled the [Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022](/wiki/Dissolution_and_Calling_of_Parliament_Act_2022 "Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022").[[54]](#cite_note-69)[[55]](#cite_note-70) This entered into force on 24 March 2022. Thus, the prime minister can again request the monarch to dissolve Parliament and call an early election with 25 working days' notice. Section 4 of the Act provided: "If it has not been dissolved earlier, a Parliament dissolves at the beginning of the day that is the fifth anniversary of the day on which it first met". The [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)") confirmed that the 2019 Parliament would, therefore, have to be dissolved, at the latest, by 17 December 2024, and that the next general election had to take place no later than 28 January 2025.[[56]](#cite_note-71)[[57]](#cite_note-72)

With no election date fixed in law, there was speculation as to when the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak"), would call an election. On 18 December 2023, Sunak told journalists that the election would take place in 2024 rather than January 2025.[[58]](#cite_note-73) On 4 January, he first suggested the general election would probably be in the second half of 2024.[[59]](#cite_note-74) Throughout 2024, political commentators and MPs expected the election to be held in the autumn.[[60]](#cite_note-75)[[61]](#cite_note-76)[[62]](#cite_note-77) On 22 May 2024, following much speculation through the day (including being asked about it by [Stephen Flynn](/wiki/Stephen_Flynn "Stephen Flynn") at [Prime Minister's Questions](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Questions "Prime Minister's Questions")),[[63]](#cite_note-78)[[64]](#cite_note-79)[[65]](#cite_note-80) Sunak officially announced the election would be held on 4 July with the dissolution of the Parliament on 30 May.[[66]](#cite_note-81)

The deadline for candidate nominations was 7 June 2024, with political campaigning for four weeks until polling day on 4 July. On the day of the election, polling stations across the country were open from 7 am, and closed at 10 pm. The date chosen for the 2024 general election made it the first to be held in July since the [1945 general election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") almost exactly seventy-nine years earlier. A total of 4,515 candidates were nominated, more than in any previous general election.[[67]](#cite_note-82)

## Electoral system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Electoral system")]

See also: [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Act_2011 "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011") and [Politics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom")

General elections in the United Kingdom are organised using [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting"). The Conservative Party, which won a majority at the 2019 general election, included pledges in its manifesto to remove the 15-year limit on voting for British citizens living abroad, and to introduce a [voter identification requirement](/wiki/Voter_identification_requirement "Voter identification requirement") in Great Britain.[[80]](#cite_note-95) These changes were included in the [Elections Act 2022](/wiki/Elections_Act_2022 "Elections Act 2022").[[81]](#cite_note-96)

### Boundary reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Boundary reviews")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Brookfield_Primary_School_as_a_polling_station.jpg/250px-Brookfield_Primary_School_as_a_polling_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brookfield_Primary_School_as_a_polling_station.jpg)

A school being used as a polling station on election day in the constituency of [Hampstead and Highgate](/wiki/Hampstead_and_Highgate "Hampstead and Highgate"). The polling station had been in a different constituency before the boundary review.

The [Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2013_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2013 periodic review of Westminster constituencies"), which proposed reducing the number of constituencies from 650 to 600, commenced in 2011 but was paused in January 2013. Following the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), each of the four parliamentary [boundary commissions of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Boundary commissions of the United Kingdom") recommenced their review process in April 2016.[[82]](#cite_note-97)[[83]](#cite_note-98)[[84]](#cite_note-99) The four commissions submitted their final recommendations to the Secretary of State on 5 September 2018[[85]](#cite_note-BCNI-2016-100)[[86]](#cite_note-101) and made their reports public a week later.[[87]](#cite_note-102)[[88]](#cite_note-103)[[89]](#cite_note-104)[[85]](#cite_note-BCNI-2016-100) However, the proposals were never put forward for approval before the calling of the general election held on 12 December 2019, and in December 2020 the reviews were formally abandoned under the Schedule to the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020.[[90]](#cite_note-PCA-2020-105) A projection by [psephologists](/wiki/Psephologists "Psephologists") [Colin Rallings](/wiki/Colin_Rallings "Colin Rallings") and [Michael Thrasher](/wiki/Michael_Thrasher "Michael Thrasher") of how the 2017 votes would have translated to seats under the 2018 boundaries suggested the changes would have been beneficial to the Conservatives and detrimental to Labour.[[91]](#cite_note-106)[[92]](#cite_note-107)

In March 2020, Cabinet Office minister [Chloe Smith](/wiki/Chloe_Smith "Chloe Smith") confirmed that the [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies") would be based on retaining 650 seats.[[93]](#cite_note-108)[[94]](#cite_note-109) The previous relevant legislation was amended by the [Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020](/wiki/Parliamentary_Constituencies_Act_2020 "Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020")[[95]](#cite_note-110) and the four boundary commissions formally launched their 2023 reviews on 5 January 2021.[[96]](#cite_note-111)[[97]](#cite_note-112)[[98]](#cite_note-113)[[99]](#cite_note-114) They were required to issue their final reports prior to 1 July 2023.[[90]](#cite_note-PCA-2020-105) Once the reports had been laid before Parliament, [Orders in Council](/wiki/Orders_in_Council "Orders in Council") giving effect to the final proposals had to be made within four months, unless "there are exceptional circumstances". Prior to the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020, boundary changes could not be implemented until they were approved by both Houses of Parliament. The boundary changes were approved at a meeting of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)") on 15 November 2023[[100]](#cite_note-115) and came into force on 29 November 2023,[[101]](#cite_note-116) meaning that the election was contested on these new boundaries.[[102]](#cite_note-117)

#### Notional 2019 results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notional 2019 results")]

Main article: [2023 review of Westminster constituencies § Notional 2019 general election results](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies#Notional_2019_general_election_results "2023 review of Westminster constituencies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg/250px-2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg)

The notional results of the [2019 election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"), if they had taken place under boundaries recommended by the Sixth Periodic Review

The election was contested under new constituency boundaries established by the [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies"). Consequently, media outlets reported seat gains and losses as compared to [notional results](/wiki/Notional_results "Notional results"). These are estimates of the results if all votes cast in 2019 were unchanged, but regrouped by the new constituency boundaries.[[103]](#cite_note-118) Since notional results in the vote counts at parliamentary elections in the UK do not yield figures at any level more specific than that of the whole constituency,[[104]](#cite_note-RecSwing124-119) it is only possible to estimate the notional results, with the assistance of local election results.

In [England](/wiki/England "England"), seats were redistributed towards [Southern England](/wiki/Southern_England "Southern England"), away from [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), due to the different rates of population growth. [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") and [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") lost two seats each, whereas [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England") gained seven and [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England") gained three.[[105]](#cite_note-120) Based on historical voting patterns, this was expected to help the Conservatives. Using the new boundaries, different parties would have won several constituencies with unchanged names but changed boundaries [from the 2019 election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_England "2019 United Kingdom general election in England"). For example, the Conservatives would have won [Wirral West](/wiki/Wirral_West "Wirral West") and [Leeds North West](/wiki/Leeds_North_West "Leeds North West") instead of the Labour Party, but Labour would have won [Pudsey](/wiki/Pudsey_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Pudsey (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Heywood & Middleton](/wiki/Heywood_%26_Middleton "Heywood & Middleton") instead of the Conservatives. [Westmorland and Lonsdale](/wiki/Westmorland_and_Lonsdale "Westmorland and Lonsdale"), the constituency represented by former Liberal Democrat leader [Tim Farron](/wiki/Tim_Farron "Tim Farron"), was notionally a Conservative seat.[[106]](#cite_note-121)[[107]](#cite_note-122)

In [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), 57 MPs were elected, down from the [59 in 2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Scotland "2019 United Kingdom general election in Scotland"),[[108]](#cite_note-123) with the following notional partisan composition of Scotland's parliamentary delegation. The Scottish National Party would have remained steady on 48 seats despite two of its constituencies being dissolved. The [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives")' seat count of six would likewise remained unchanged. [Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") would have retained [Edinburgh South](/wiki/Edinburgh_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh South (UK Parliament constituency)"), the sole constituency they won in 2019. Had the 2019 general election occurred with the new boundaries in effect, the [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") would have only won two seats ([Edinburgh West](/wiki/Edinburgh_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh West (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Orkney and Shetland](/wiki/Orkney_and_Shetland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney and Shetland (UK Parliament constituency)")), instead of the four they did win that year, as the expanded electorates in the other two would overcome their slender majorities.[[109]](#cite_note-124)

Under the new boundaries, [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") lost eight seats, electing 32 MPs instead of the 40 it [elected in 2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Wales "2019 United Kingdom general election in Wales"). [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") would have won 18 instead of the 22 MPs it elected in 2019, and the [Welsh Conservatives](/wiki/Welsh_Conservatives "Welsh Conservatives") 12 instead of 14. Due to the abolition and merging of rural constituencies in [West Wales](/wiki/West_Wales "West Wales"), [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") would have only won two seats instead of four. Nonetheless, the boundary changes were expected to cause difficulty for the Conservatives as more pro-Labour areas were added to some of their strongest seats.[[110]](#cite_note-125)

In [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), the notional results are identical to the actual results of the [2019 general election in Northern Ireland](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Northern_Ireland "2019 United Kingdom general election in Northern Ireland").[[111]](#cite_note-126)

## Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Campaign")]

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Overview")]

Major party election spending[[112]](#cite_note-elcom-20250717-127)

| Party | £ (millions) | Percent of total |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Labour | 30.1 | 31.8% |
| Conservative and Unionist | 23.9 | 25.3% |
| Liberal Democrats | 5.6 | 6.0% |
| Reform UK | 5.5 | 5.8% |
| Green | 1.7 | 1.8% |
| Scottish National | 0.8 | 0.8% |
| Co-operative | 0.3 | 0.3% |
| Total (all party, non-party and candidate) | 94.5 |

Labour entered the election with a large lead over the Conservatives in [opinion polls](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Opinion polling for the 2024 United Kingdom general election"), and the potential scale of the party's victory was a topic of discussion during the campaign period.[[113]](#cite_note-Walker_202423-128)[[114]](#cite_note-Hunt_202423-129) The [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom"), [healthcare](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"), [education](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Education in the United Kingdom"), infrastructure development, environment, housing, [energy](/wiki/Energy_in_the_United_Kingdom "Energy in the United Kingdom"), and [immigration](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom"), and standards in public office were main campaign topics. The Conservative campaign led by [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") focused primarily on attacks towards Labour over alleged tax plans including a – robustly disputed – claim that Labour would cost households £2,000 more in tax.[[115]](#cite_note-130)[[116]](#cite_note-131)

[Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") used the word "change" as his campaign slogan and offered voters the chance to "turn the page" by voting Labour.[[117]](#cite_note-132) The [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") campaign led by [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey") was dominated by his campaign stunts, which were used to bring attention to campaign topics.[[118]](#cite_note-133)[[119]](#cite_note-134) When asked about these stunts, Davey said: "Politicians need to take the concerns and interests of voters seriously but I'm not sure they need to take themselves seriously all the time and I'm quite happy to have some fun."[[120]](#cite_note-Patrick-135) Party manifesto and fiscal spending plans were independently analysed by the [Institute for Fiscal Studies](/wiki/Institute_for_Fiscal_Studies "Institute for Fiscal Studies")[[121]](#cite_note-136) and their environmental policies were assessed by [Friends of the Earth](/wiki/Friends_of_the_Earth "Friends of the Earth").[[122]](#cite_note-137)

### Announcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Announcement")]

On the afternoon of 22 May 2024, Sunak announced that he had asked the King to call a general election for 4 July 2024, surprising his own MPs.[[123]](#cite_note-138) Though Sunak had the option to wait until December 2024 to call the election, he said that he decided on the date because he believed that the economy was improving, and that "falling inflation and net migration figures would reinforce the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)")'s election message of 'sticking to the plan'".[[124]](#cite_note-139) The calling of the election was welcomed by all major parties.[[125]](#cite_note-140)

Sunak's announcement took place during heavy rain at a lectern outside [10 Downing Street](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street"), without the use of any shelter from the downpour.[[126]](#cite_note-141) The [D:Ream](/wiki/D_Ream "D Ream") song "[Things Can Only Get Better](/wiki/Things_Can_Only_Get_Better_(D:Ream_song) "Things Can Only Get Better (D:Ream song)")" (previously used by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") in its successful [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election") campaign) was being played loudly in the background by the political activist [Steve Bray](/wiki/Steve_Bray "Steve Bray") as Sunak announced the date of the general election.[[127]](#cite_note-142) This led to the song reaching number two on UK's [iTunes Charts](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store").[[128]](#cite_note-143)[[129]](#cite_note-144)

### 22–29 May

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 22–29 May")]

At the beginning of the campaign, Labour had a significant lead in polling over the Conservatives.[[24]](#cite_note-econpredict0424-31)[[130]](#cite_note-145) Polling also showed Labour doing well against the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) in Scotland.[[131]](#cite_note-146) When visiting [Windermere](/wiki/Windermere "Windermere"), Davey fell off a [paddleboard](/wiki/Paddleboarding "Paddleboarding"), while campaigning to highlight the issue of sewage discharges into rivers and lakes.[[132]](#cite_note-147) A couple of days later, Davey won media attention when going down a [Slip 'N Slide](/wiki/Slip_%27N_Slide "Slip 'N Slide"), while drawing attention to deteriorating mental health among children.[[120]](#cite_note-Patrick-135)

On 23 May, Sunak said that before the election there would be [no flights to Rwanda for those seeking asylum](/wiki/Rwanda_asylum_plan "Rwanda asylum plan").[[133]](#cite_note-148) Immigration figures were published for 2023 showing immigration remained at historically high levels, but had fallen compared to 2022.[[134]](#cite_note-149) [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") announced that he would not be standing in the general election, preferring to focus on campaigning for the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") instead.[[135]](#cite_note-150) However, Farage later announced on 3 June that, contrary to his statement earlier in the campaign, he would [stand for Parliament](/wiki/Clacton_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Clacton in the 2024 United Kingdom general election") in [Clacton](/wiki/Clacton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Clacton (UK Parliament constituency)"), and that he had resumed leadership of [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"), taking over from [Richard Tice](/wiki/Richard_Tice "Richard Tice"), who remained the party's chairman. Farage also predicted that Labour would win the election, whilst stating the Conservatives were incapable of being the Official Opposition due to having spent "much of the last five years fighting each other rather than fighting for the interests of this country."[[136]](#cite_note-BBCFarageR-151) Also on 23 May, Davey launched the Liberal Democrat campaign in [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cheltenham (UK Parliament constituency)") in [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire").[[137]](#cite_note-152) The SNP campaign launch the same day was overshadowed by a dispute around leader [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney")'s support for embattled MSP [Michael Matheson](/wiki/Michael_Matheson_(politician) "Michael Matheson (politician)") and developments in [Operation Branchform](/wiki/Operation_Branchform "Operation Branchform"), the investigation into potential misuse of campaign funds by [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon") and her husband.[[138]](#cite_note-153) Starmer launched the Labour Party campaign in [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_and_Rainham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Gillingham and Rainham (UK Parliament constituency)") at the [Priestfield Stadium](/wiki/Priestfield_Stadium "Priestfield Stadium").[[139]](#cite_note-154)

On 24 May, the Conservatives proposed setting up a [Royal Commission](/wiki/Royal_Commission "Royal Commission") to consider a form of mandatory national service.[[140]](#cite_note-155) It would be made up of two streams for 18-year-olds to choose from, either 'community volunteering' by volunteering with organisations such as the NHS, fire service, ambulance, search and rescue, and critical local infrastructure, or 'military training' in areas like logistics and cyber security.[[141]](#cite_note-156) Former Labour leader [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") announced on 24 May he was [running as an independent](/wiki/Islington_North_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Islington North in the 2024 United Kingdom general election") in [Islington North](/wiki/Islington_North "Islington North") against a Labour candidate, and was thus expelled from the party.[[142]](#cite_note-157)

On 27 May, Starmer made a keynote speech on security and other issues.[[143]](#cite_note-158)[[144]](#cite_note-159) On 28 May, the Conservatives pledged a "Triple Lock Plus" where the personal income tax allowance for pensioners would always stay higher than the state pension.[[145]](#cite_note-160)[[146]](#cite_note-161) Davey went [paddleboarding](/wiki/Paddleboarding "Paddleboarding") on [Lake Windermere](/wiki/Lake_Windermere "Lake Windermere") in the marginal constituency of [Westmorland and Lonsdale](/wiki/Westmorland_and_Lonsdale "Westmorland and Lonsdale"), highlighting the release of sewage in waterways.[[147]](#cite_note-162) He pledged to abolish [Ofwat](/wiki/Ofwat "Ofwat") and introduce a new water regulator to tackle the situation, in addition to proposing a ban on bonuses for chief executives of water companies.[[148]](#cite_note-163) Starmer was in [West Sussex](/wiki/West_Sussex "West Sussex") and emphasised his small town roots in his first big campaign speech.[[149]](#cite_note-164)

On 29 May, Labour's [Wes Streeting](/wiki/Wes_Streeting "Wes Streeting") promised a 18-week NHS waiting target within five years of a Labour government.[[150]](#cite_note-165) Labour also pledged to double the number of NHS scanners in England. On the same day Starmer denied that [Diane Abbott](/wiki/Diane_Abbott "Diane Abbott") had been blocked as a candidate amid differing reports.[[151]](#cite_note-166) Abbott had been elected as a Labour MP, but had been suspended from the parliamentary party for a brief period. There was controversy about further Labour Party candidate selections, with several candidates on the left of the party being excluded.[[152]](#cite_note-167) Abbott said she had been barred from standing as a Labour Party candidate at the election, but Starmer later said she would be "free" to stand as a Labour candidate.[[153]](#cite_note-168)

### 30 May – 5 June

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 30 May – 5 June")]

On 30 May, both the Conservatives and Labour ruled out any rise in [value-added tax](/wiki/Value-added_tax_in_the_United_Kingdom "Value-added tax in the United Kingdom").[[154]](#cite_note-169) The SNPs [Màiri McAllan](/wiki/M%C3%A0iri_McAllan "Màiri McAllan") claimed that only the SNP offered Scotland a route back into the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), making [Pro-Europeanism](/wiki/Pro-Europeanism "Pro-Europeanism") part of the party's campaign.[[155]](#cite_note-170) Reform UK proposed an [immigration tax](/wiki/Immigration_tax "Immigration tax") on British firms who employ [foreign workers](/wiki/Foreign_worker "Foreign worker").[[156]](#cite_note-171) [Carla Denyer](/wiki/Carla_Denyer "Carla Denyer") and [Adrian Ramsay](/wiki/Adrian_Ramsay "Adrian Ramsay") launched the [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") campaign in Bristol.[[157]](#cite_note-172) [Rhun ap Iorwerth](/wiki/Rhun_ap_Iorwerth "Rhun ap Iorwerth") launched the [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") campaign in [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_Gwynedd "Bangor, Gwynedd").[[158]](#cite_note-173) [George Galloway](/wiki/George_Galloway "George Galloway") launched the [Workers Party of Britain](/wiki/Workers_Party_of_Britain "Workers Party of Britain") campaign in [Ashton-under-Lyne](/wiki/Ashton-under-Lyne "Ashton-under-Lyne").[[159]](#cite_note-174)

On 31 May, the Conservatives announced new "pride in places" pledges, including new rules to tackle anti-social behaviour, rolling out the hot-spot policing programme to more areas, and more town regeneration projects. The Conservatives also unveiled plans for fly-tippers to get points on their driving licences and other new measures to protect the environment.[[160]](#cite_note-175)

On 2 June, Labour pledged to reduce record high [legal immigration to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom") by improving training for British workers.[[161]](#cite_note-176) Net migration to the UK was 685,000 in 2023.[[162]](#cite_note-177)[[163]](#cite_note-178)[[164]](#cite_note-179) Labour also focused on national security, with Starmer reaffirming his commitment to a "nuclear deterrent triple lock", including building four new nuclear submarines.[[136]](#cite_note-BBCFarageR-151) A YouGov poll conducted on the same day put Labour on course for the party's biggest election victory in history, beating [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair")'s [1997 landslide](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election").[[165]](#cite_note-180)

On 4 June, Farage launched his campaign in Clacton.[[166]](#cite_note-181) He predicted the previous day that Reform UK would be the [Official Opposition](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Most_Loyal_Opposition "His Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition") following the election as opposed to the Conservatives, saying that the Conservatives are incapable of being the Opposition due to "spending most of the last five years fighting each other rather than fighting for the interests of this country".[[167]](#cite_note-182)

### 6–12 June

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 6–12 June")]

On 6 June, the Green Party announced plans to invest an extra £50 billion a year for the NHS by raising taxes on the top 1% of earners.[[168]](#cite_note-183) The Conservatives announced a policy on expanding [child benefit](/wiki/Child_benefit "Child benefit") for higher-earners.[[169]](#cite_note-184) Labour also announced communities will be given powers to transform derelict areas into parks and green spaces. Labour's countryside protection plan would also include the planting new national forests, taskforces for tree-planting and flood resilience, new river pathways, and a commitment to revive nature.[[170]](#cite_note-185) Green spaces would be a requirement in the development of new housing and town plans.[[171]](#cite_note-186)

Both Sunak and Starmer attended D-Day commemorations in Normandy on 6 June, the 80th anniversary of [Operation Neptune](/wiki/Operation_Neptune "Operation Neptune"). Sunak was widely criticised for leaving events early to do an interview with ITV, including by veterans.[[172]](#cite_note-187) Starmer met with [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy") and [King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III") during the D-Day commemorations, and said that Sunak "has to answer for his actions".[[173]](#cite_note-188)[[174]](#cite_note-189) Sunak apologised the next day[[175]](#cite_note-190) and apologised again on 10 June.[[176]](#cite_note-191) He made a third apology on 12 June.[[177]](#cite_note-192)

Farage was among those critical of Sunak over his leaving the D-Day events,[[178]](#cite_note-193) saying on 7 June that Sunak did not understand "our culture". Conservative and Labour politicians criticised these words as being a racist attack on Sunak, which Farage denied.[[179]](#cite_note-194) On 10 June, Labour pledged 100,000 new childcare places and more than 3,000 new nurseries as part of its childcare plan.[[180]](#cite_note-195) It also announced its Child Health Action Plan, which included providing every school with a qualified mental health counsellor, boosting preventative mental health services, transforming NHS dentistry, legislating for a progressive ban on smoking (a bill proposed by the Conservative government which had not passed before Parliament was prorogued),[[181]](#cite_note-196) banning junk food advertising to children, and banning energy drinks for under 16s.[[182]](#cite_note-197)[[183]](#cite_note-198)

Labour released its plan for small business on 8 June, which included pledges to ⁠replace the current business rates system and to tackle the skill shortage by creating a new public body, [Skills England](/wiki/Skills_England "Skills England").[[184]](#cite_note-199) They also pledged to ⁠revitalise Britain's high streets and a new trade strategy.[[185]](#cite_note-200)

The Liberal Democrat manifesto *[For a Fair Deal](/wiki/For_a_Fair_Deal "For a Fair Deal")* was released on 10 June,[[186]](#cite_note-201)[[187]](#cite_note-202) which included commitments on [free personal care](/wiki/National_Care_Service "National Care Service") in England,[[188]](#cite_note-203) investment in the NHS including more [GPs](/wiki/General_practitioner "General practitioner"), increased funding for education and childcare (including a tutoring guarantee for children from low-income families), increased funding for public services, tax reforms, reaching [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") by 2045 (5 years before the current government target of 2050), investing in green infrastructure, innovation, training and skills across the UK to boost economic growth, and removing the two-child limit on [tax](/wiki/Child_Tax_Credit#United_Kingdom "Child Tax Credit") and [benefits](/wiki/Universal_Credit "Universal Credit").[[189]](#cite_note-204) The Liberal Democrats also offered a lifelong skills grant, giving adults £5,000 to spend on improving their skills.[[190]](#cite_note-205) The party wants electoral reform, and pledged to introduce [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") for electing MPs, and local councillors in England, and cap donations to political parties.[[191]](#cite_note-206)[[192]](#cite_note-207)

Sunak released the Conservative manifesto *[Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.](/wiki/Clear_Plan._Bold_Action._Secure_Future. "Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.")* on 11 June, addressing the economy, taxes, welfare, expanding free childcare, education, healthcare, environment, energy, transport, community, and crime.[[193]](#cite_note-208)[[194]](#cite_note-209) They pledged to lower taxes, increase education and NHS spending, deliver 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors, introduce a new model of [National Service](/wiki/National_Service_in_the_United_Kingdom "National Service in the United Kingdom"), continue to expand apprenticeships and vocational training, simplify the planning system to speed up infrastructure projects (digital, transport and energy), and to treble Britain's offshore wind capacity and support solar energy. The manifesto includes a pledge to abolish Stamp Duty on homes worth up to £425,000 for first time buyers and expand the [Help to Buy](/wiki/Help_to_Buy "Help to Buy") scheme.[[195]](#cite_note-210) The Conservatives also pledged a recruitment of 8,000 new police officers and a rollout of [facial recognition technology](/wiki/Facial_recognition_technology "Facial recognition technology").[[196]](#cite_note-211) Much of what has been proposed is already incorporated in the [2023](/wiki/2023_United_Kingdom_budget "2023 United Kingdom budget") and [2024 budget](/wiki/March_2024_United_Kingdom_budget "March 2024 United Kingdom budget").[[197]](#cite_note-212)[[198]](#cite_note-213)[[199]](#cite_note-214)

[Carla Denyer](/wiki/Carla_Denyer "Carla Denyer") and [Adrian Ramsay](/wiki/Adrian_Ramsay "Adrian Ramsay") released the Green Party's manifesto *Real Hope. Real Change.* on 12 June, which pledged more taxes on the highest earners, generating £70 billion a year to help tackle climate change and the NHS. They also pledged increased spending for public services, free personal care in England, [renationalisation](/wiki/Renationalisation "Renationalisation") of railway, water and energy, a green society, a [wealth tax](/wiki/Wealth_tax "Wealth tax"), a carbon tax, and a windfall tax on the profit of banks.[[200]](#cite_note-215)[[201]](#cite_note-216) The manifesto promises quicker access to NHS dentistry and GPs and reductions in the hospital waiting list. They would also reach [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") by 2040 and introduce rent controls.[[202]](#cite_note-217)[[203]](#cite_note-218)

On 12 June Conservative minister [Grant Shapps](/wiki/Grant_Shapps "Grant Shapps") said in a radio interview that voters should support the Conservatives so as to prevent Labour winning "a super-majority", meaning a large majority (the UK Parliament does not have any formal [supermajority](/wiki/Supermajority "Supermajority") rules). This was interpreted by journalists as a possible and surprising admission of defeat.[[204]](#cite_note-219)[[205]](#cite_note-220)[[206]](#cite_note-221) It paralleled social media advertising by the Conservatives that also focused on urging votes not to give Starmer a large majority.[[207]](#cite_note-222)

### 13–19 June

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 13–19 June")]

On 13 June, Starmer released the Labour Party manifesto *[Change](/wiki/Change_(manifesto) "Change (manifesto)")*, which focused on economic growth, planning system reforms, infrastructure, clean energy, healthcare, education, childcare, crime, and strengthening workers' rights.[[208]](#cite_note-223)[[209]](#cite_note-224) It pledged a new publicly owned energy company ([Great British Energy](/wiki/Great_British_Energy "Great British Energy")) and [National Wealth Fund](/wiki/National_Wealth_Fund "National Wealth Fund"), a "Green Prosperity Plan", rebuilding the NHS and reducing patient waiting times, free breakfast clubs in primary schools, investing in green infrastructure, innovation, training and skills across the UK to boost economic growth, and renationalisation of the [railway network](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Great_Britain "Rail transport in Great Britain") ([Great British Railways](/wiki/Great_British_Railways "Great British Railways")).[[210]](#cite_note-225) It includes wealth creation and "pro-business and pro-worker" policies.[[211]](#cite_note-226) The manifesto also pledged to give votes to 16-year olds, reform the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), and to tax private schools, with money generated going into improving state education.[[212]](#cite_note-227)[[213]](#cite_note-228)[[214]](#cite_note-229) The party guaranteed giving all areas of England [devolution powers](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), in areas such as integrated transport, planning, skills, and health.[[215]](#cite_note-230)[[216]](#cite_note-231)

On 17 June, Farage and Tice released the Reform UK manifesto, which they called a "contract" (*Our Contract with You*). It pledged to lower taxes, lower immigration, increase funding for public services, reform the NHS and decrease its waiting lists down to zero, bring utilities and critical national infrastructure under 50% public ownership (the other 50% owned by [pension funds](/wiki/Pension_funds "Pension funds")), replace the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") with a more democratic second chamber, and to replace [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") with a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").[[217]](#cite_note-232) It also pledged to accelerate transport infrastructure in coastal regions, Wales, the North, and the Midlands.[[218]](#cite_note-233)[[219]](#cite_note-234) The party also wants to freeze non-essential immigration and recruit 40,000 new police officers.[[220]](#cite_note-235) Reform UK are the only major party to oppose the current [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") target made by the government.[[221]](#cite_note-236) Instead, it pledged to support the environment with more tree planting, more recycling and less single-use plastics.[[222]](#cite_note-237)[[223]](#cite_note-238)[[224]](#cite_note-239) Farage predicted Labour would win the election, but said he was planning to campaign for the next election.[[225]](#cite_note-240)

Labour's [Rachel Reeves](/wiki/Rachel_Reeves "Rachel Reeves") claimed Labour's green plans would create over 650,000 jobs.[[226]](#cite_note-241)[[227]](#cite_note-242) The Liberal Democrats offered more cost-of-living help for rural communities.[[228]](#cite_note-243) Davey highlighted his manifesto pledge to build 380,000 new homes a year, 150,000 of which would be social homes.[[229]](#cite_note-244) On 18 June, Labour pledged hundreds of new banking hubs, to ''breathe life'' into high streets.[[230]](#cite_note-245)[[231]](#cite_note-246) Labour also promised a large increase of renewable energy jobs, backed by new green apprenticeships.[[232]](#cite_note-247)

On 19 June, both the SNP and Sinn Féin released their manifestos. Swinney said a vote for his party would "intensify" the pressure to secure a [second Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/Second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Second Scottish independence referendum"), with other pledges in the SNP manifesto including boosting NHS funding, scrapping the two-child limit on benefits, calling for an immediate ceasefire in the Gaza Strip, scrapping the [Trident defence programme](/wiki/Trident_(UK_nuclear_programme) "Trident (UK nuclear programme)"), re-joining the European Union, transitioning to a green economy attracting more foreign migrants,[[233]](#cite_note-248) tackling drug deaths and [devolving](/wiki/Devolving "Devolving") broadcasting powers.[[234]](#cite_note-249) The Sinn Féin manifesto called for greater [devolution to Northern Ireland](/wiki/Devolution_to_Northern_Ireland "Devolution to Northern Ireland") and for the UK and Irish governments to set a date for a referendum on the [unification of Northern Ireland with the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Unification_of_Ireland "Unification of Ireland").[[235]](#cite_note-250)

Galloway released the Workers Party manifesto, with promises to improve "poverty pay" and provide more social housing.[[236]](#cite_note-251) It pledged the renationalisation of utility companies, free school meals for all children without means testing, free adult education, and to hold a referendum on the continued existence of the monarchy and proportional representation for elections.[[237]](#cite_note-252)

[David T. C. Davies](/wiki/David_T._C._Davies "David T. C. Davies"), the [Secretary of State for Wales](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Wales "Secretary of State for Wales"), told a BBC interview the polls were "clearly pointing at a large Labour majority", but added that he believed there was "no great optimism" from voters.[[238]](#cite_note-253) A potentially large Labour majority was also acknowledged by [Jeremy Hunt](/wiki/Jeremy_Hunt "Jeremy Hunt"), the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), and [Mel Stride](/wiki/Mel_Stride "Mel Stride"), the [Secretary of State for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Work_and_Pensions "Secretary of State for Work and Pensions").[[239]](#cite_note-254) Alison McGarry, the Labour chair of Islington North, resigned from the Labour Party after being spotted campaigning for Corbyn; she resigned rather than face expulsion for breaking the party's rules on campaigning for a rival candidate.[[240]](#cite_note-255)

### 20–26 June

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 20–26 June")]

On 20 June, the parties focused on housing. Labour pledged action to protect renters with new legal protections for tenants. It would immediately ban [Section 21 "no-fault" evictions](/wiki/Section_21_notice "Section 21 notice"), as part of plans to reform the private rented sector in England.[[241]](#cite_note-256) Labour also pledged to reform [planning laws](/wiki/Planning_law "Planning law") and build 1.5 million homes to spread homeownership.[[242]](#cite_note-257) They would also ensure new housing is supported by new local infrastructure (public transport, healthcare, schools), and pledged to build more housing near railway stations and on the 'grey belt' (disused car parks or industrial estates).[[243]](#cite_note-258) The Conservatives offered stronger legal protections for tenants, including banning Section 21 "no-fault" evictions.[[244]](#cite_note-259) They said they would build 1.6 million new homes (including social housing) with new local infrastructure, prioritising [brownfield development](/wiki/Brownfield_land#United_Kingdom "Brownfield land"), while protecting the countryside.[[245]](#cite_note-260) The Liberal Democrats offered more protections for tenants, additional social housing, and more [garden cities](/wiki/Garden_cities "Garden cities").[[246]](#cite_note-261)[[247]](#cite_note-262)

Also on 20 June, the Alliance Party in Northern Ireland launched their manifesto.[[248]](#cite_note-263) Its core policies include reforming the political institutions, dedicated funding for [integrated education](/wiki/Integrated_education "Integrated education"), a Green New Deal to decarbonise Northern Ireland's economy, childcare reforms, and lowering the voting age to 16.[[249]](#cite_note-264)

On 21 June, in an interview, Farage repeated comments he had made previously stating that the West and NATO provoked [Russia's invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russia%27s_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russia's invasion of Ukraine"). He was criticised for this by Sunak and Starmer.[[250]](#cite_note-265) He also stated that Reform UK would lower the tax burden to encourage people into work.[[251]](#cite_note-266) Farage stated in another interview that he would remove university tuition fees if he won power for those studying science, technology, engineering, medicine or maths. Reform UK have already pledged to scrap interest on student loans and to extend the loan capital repayment periods to 45 years.[[252]](#cite_note-267) Farage also declared his ambition for Reform UK to replace the Conservatives as the biggest right-wing party in Parliament.[[253]](#cite_note-268)

The Conservatives pledged a review of licensing laws and planning rules aimed at boosting pubs, restaurants and music venues.[[254]](#cite_note-269) Labour framed its 10-year science and [R&D](/wiki/R%26D "R&D") budget plans as part of its industrial strategy, with an aim of boosting workforce and regional development.[[255]](#cite_note-270)[[256]](#cite_note-271) Labour and the Liberal Democrats also focused on water pollution and improving England's water quality.[[257]](#cite_note-272)[[258]](#cite_note-273) Labour pledged to put failing water companies who do not meet ''high environmental standards'' under [special measures](/wiki/Special_measures "Special measures"), give regulators new powers to block the payment of bonuses to executives who pollute waterways, and criminal charges against persistent law breakers. They also ensured independent monitoring of every outlet.[[259]](#cite_note-274)

On 24 June, Labour focused on NHS dentistry and health, pledged to hold a knife crime summit every year and halve incidents within a decade.[[260]](#cite_note-275)[[261]](#cite_note-276) The Greens pledged to end 'dental deserts' with £3 billion for new NHS contracts.[[262]](#cite_note-277)

The Liberal Democrats launched a mini-manifesto for [carers](/wiki/Carers "Carers").[[263]](#cite_note-278) It pledged to establish an independent living taskforce to help people live independently in their own homes, a new care worker's minimum wage to raise their pay by £2 an hour, and a new National Care Agency. Sunak released the Scottish Conservatives' manifesto.[[264]](#cite_note-279) Starmer discussed a proposed Football Governance Bill,[[265]](#cite_note-280) which will establish the new Independent Football Regulator.[[266]](#cite_note-281) The Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have also committed to introducing an Independent Football Regulator.[[267]](#cite_note-282) The Liberal Democrats pledged to establish a series of "creative enterprise zones" across the UK to regenerate cultural output.[[268]](#cite_note-283)

On 26 June, [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond") released the [Alba Party](/wiki/Alba_Party "Alba Party") manifesto. It pledged to increase funding for public services, increase NHS staffing, provide an annual £500 payment to households receiving the council tax reduction at a cost of £250 million, increase the Scottish Child Payment, reducing fuel bills, a new Scottish clean energy public company, and Scottish Independence.[[269]](#cite_note-284)

Starmer pledged GP reforms, including the training of thousands more GPs, updating the NHS App, and bringing back the '[family doctor](/wiki/Family_doctor "Family doctor")'.[[270]](#cite_note-285) Labour would also trial new "neighbourhood health centres".[[271]](#cite_note-286) The [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") also launched their manifesto on 26 June in Northern Ireland.[[272]](#cite_note-287) It pledged a 'Marshall Plan' to tackle health, institutional reform, stronger environmental protection with an independent Environmental Protection Agency, and improving NI's financial settlement.[[273]](#cite_note-288)

### 27 June – 4 July

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 27 June – 4 July")]

On 27 June, Labour pledged to reform careers advice and work experience in schools for one million pupils, committing to deliver two weeks' worth of quality work experience for every young person, and recruit more than thousands of new careers advisers.[[274]](#cite_note-289) This is part of the party's wider plan to establish a "youth guarantee" of access to training, an apprenticeship or support to find work for all 18 to 21-year-olds.[[275]](#cite_note-290)[[276]](#cite_note-291)

On 27 June, an undercover [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") journalist secretly recorded members of Farage's campaign team using offensive racial, Islamophobic and homophobic language, also suggesting [refugees](/wiki/English_Channel_illegal_migrant_crossings_(2018%E2%80%93present) "English Channel illegal migrant crossings (2018–present)") should be used as "target practice".[[277]](#cite_note-BBC_racist-292) In a statement, Farage said that he was "dismayed" at the "reprehensible" language.[[278]](#cite_note-293) Tice said that racist comments were "inappropriate".[[277]](#cite_note-BBC_racist-292) Farage later accused Channel 4 of a "set-up", stating that one of the canvassers, Andrew Parker, had been an actor. Farage stated that Parker had been "acting from the moment he came into the office", and cited video of Parker performing "rough-speaking" from his acting website. Channel 4 denied that Parker was known to them prior to the report.[[279]](#cite_note-294) Regarding other members of his campaign team, Farage stated that the individuals in question had "watched England play football, they were in the pub, they were drunk, it was crass."[[280]](#cite_note-295)

On 29 June, the Liberal Democrats called for an 'emergency NHS budget' to hire more GPs.[[281]](#cite_note-296) Starmer hosted a major campaign rally,[[282]](#cite_note-news.sky.com-297) and stated in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* "if you vote Labour on Thursday, the work of change begins. We will launch a new national mission to create wealth in every community. We'll get to work on repairing our public services with an immediate cash injection, alongside urgent reforms. And we will break with recent years by always putting country before party".[[282]](#cite_note-news.sky.com-297)[[283]](#cite_note-298)

The Greens announced a 'Charter for Small Business', which pledged £2 billion per year in grant funding for local authorities, regional mutual banks for investment in decarbonisation and local economic sustainability, and increasing annual public subsidies for rail and bus travel to £10 billion.[[284]](#cite_note-299)[[285]](#cite_note-300) They also pledged free bus travel for under-18s.[[286]](#cite_note-301) The [Northern Ireland Conservatives](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Conservatives "Northern Ireland Conservatives") also launched their manifesto.[[287]](#cite_note-302) On 30 June, the Liberal Democrats pledged to double funding for [Bereavement Support Payments](/wiki/Bereavement_Support_Payment "Bereavement Support Payment"), and to spend £440 million a year on support for bereaved families.[[288]](#cite_note-303)

On 2 July, the Greens announced its £8 billion education package would include scrapping tuition fees, providing free school meals for all children, a qualified [counsellor](/wiki/School_counselor "School counselor") in every school and college, and new special needs provision. They also want to end formal testing in primary and secondary schools with a system of [continuous assessment](/wiki/Continuous_assessment "Continuous assessment").[[289]](#cite_note-304) Former prime minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") campaigned for the Conservatives.[[290]](#cite_note-305) On 3 July, the political parties made their closing arguments on the last day of campaigning, with Sunak stating he would "take full responsibility" for the result.[[291]](#cite_note-306) At the end of the campaign, Labour maintained their significant lead in polling over the Conservatives, and had [endorsements](/wiki/Endorsements_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Endorsements in the 2024 United Kingdom general election") from celebrities, including [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John").[[292]](#cite_note-307)

On 4 July, less than an hour before polls closed, Sunak's government announced the [2024 Dissolution Honours](/wiki/2024_Dissolution_Honours "2024 Dissolution Honours"), with [life peerages](/wiki/Life_peerage "Life peerage") being given to 19 people, including former prime minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") and [Cass Review](/wiki/Cass_Review "Cass Review") author [Hilary Cass](/wiki/Hilary_Cass "Hilary Cass").[[293]](#cite_note-308)[[294]](#cite_note-309)

## Betting scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Betting scandal")]

Main article: [2024 United Kingdom general election betting scandal](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election_betting_scandal "2024 United Kingdom general election betting scandal")

On 12 June 2024, it was reported that Conservative candidate and [Parliamentary Private Secretary to the Prime Minister](/wiki/Parliamentary_Private_Secretary_to_the_Prime_Minister "Parliamentary Private Secretary to the Prime Minister"), [Craig Williams](/wiki/Craig_Williams_(British_politician) "Craig Williams (British politician)") had placed a £100 bet on the date of the election being in July, three days before the date was announced. The bet was referred to the [Gambling Commission](/wiki/Gambling_Commission "Gambling Commission") to determine whether Williams had placed the bet based on confidential information, which could constitute a criminal offence.[[295]](#cite_note-310) Williams apologised for the bet, but neither he nor Sunak would answer whether he had inside information.[[296]](#cite_note-311)[[297]](#cite_note-312)

On 19 June, a police protection officer assigned to Sunak was arrested for betting on the election date.[[298]](#cite_note-313)[[299]](#cite_note-314)[[300]](#cite_note-315) On the same day, it was also announced that Laura Saunders, the Conservative candidate for [Bristol North West](/wiki/Bristol_North_West "Bristol North West"), was being investigated for betting on the election date. Her husband, Tony Lee, was also being investigated by the Gambling Commission and took a leave of absence from his role as the party's director of campaigning on 19 June.[[301]](#cite_note-316)[[302]](#cite_note-317)[[303]](#cite_note-318)

Over the following two weeks, it was reported that dozens of further Conservative Party-linked officials were being investigated by the Gambling Commission,[[304]](#cite_note-319) including Conservative Party's chief data officer, Nick Mason;[[305]](#cite_note-320) *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* reported that the investigation was being widened to hundreds of suspicious bets.[[306]](#cite_note-321) On 25 June, both Williams and Saunders had their support for their candidacies withdrawn by the Conservative Party.

In April 2025, Williams and 14 others were charged with offences under the Gambling Act 2005.[[307]](#cite_note-322)

## Debates and interviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Debates and interviews")]

### Debates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Debates")]

| [← 2019 debates](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election_debates "2019 United Kingdom general election debates") | 2024 |
| --- | --- |

[Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") challenged [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") to six televised debates.[[308]](#cite_note-323) Starmer announced that he would not agree to such a proposal, and offered two head-to-head debates—one shown on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News"), and one shown on [ITV](/wiki/ITV_News "ITV News"); a spokesperson said both networks would offer the greatest audience, and the prospect of any debates on smaller channels would be rejected as it would not be a "valuable use of campaign time". [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey") declared his wish to be included in "any televised debates", although he would ultimately only be featured in one debate.[[309]](#cite_note-324)

On 29 May, it was announced that the first leaders' debate would be hosted by ITV News with [Julie Etchingham](/wiki/Julie_Etchingham "Julie Etchingham") as moderator, on 4 June.[[310]](#cite_note-325) Key topics were the cost of living crisis, the National Health Service (NHS), young people, education, immigration and tax policy.[[311]](#cite_note-326) Sunak said that Labour would cost households £2000 more in tax, which Starmer denied. Sunak said this figure was calculated by "independent Treasury officials". Fact checkers disputed the sum, stating it was based on assumptions made by political appointees and that the figure was over a four-year period. On 5 June, the BBC reported that [James Bowler](/wiki/James_Bowler_(civil_servant) "James Bowler (civil servant)"), the Treasury permanent secretary, wrote that "civil servants were not involved in the [...] calculation of the total figure used" and that "any costings derived from other sources or produced by other organisations should not be presented as having been produced by the Civil Service".[[312]](#cite_note-327) The [Office for Statistics Regulation](/wiki/Office_for_Statistics_Regulation "Office for Statistics Regulation") also criticised the claim on the grounds that it was presented without the listener knowing it was a sum over 4 years.[[313]](#cite_note-328) A [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") snap poll after the debate indicated that 46% of debate viewers thought Sunak had performed better, and 45% believed Starmer had performed better.[[314]](#cite_note-329) A Savanta poll published the next day favoured Starmer 44% to Sunak 39%.[[315]](#cite_note-330) The debate was watched by 5.37 million viewers, making it the most-viewed programme of the week.[[316]](#cite_note-BARB-3Jun-9Jun-331)

An [STV](/wiki/STV_(TV_channel) "STV (TV channel)") debate hosted by [Colin Mackay](/wiki/Colin_Mackay_(journalist) "Colin Mackay (journalist)") took place on 3 June, which included [Douglas Ross](/wiki/Douglas_Ross_(Scottish_politician) "Douglas Ross (Scottish politician)"), [Anas Sarwar](/wiki/Anas_Sarwar "Anas Sarwar"), [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney") and [Alex Cole-Hamilton](/wiki/Alex_Cole-Hamilton "Alex Cole-Hamilton").[[317]](#cite_note-STVDebate-332) Another debate between these leaders (also including [Lorna Slater](/wiki/Lorna_Slater "Lorna Slater")) took place on 11 June, on BBC Scotland, hosted by [Stephen Jardine](/wiki/Stephen_Jardine "Stephen Jardine"). A BBC debate hosted by [Mishal Husain](/wiki/Mishal_Husain "Mishal Husain") took place on 7 June, which included [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), [Carla Denyer](/wiki/Carla_Denyer "Carla Denyer"), [Rhun ap Iorwerth](/wiki/Rhun_ap_Iorwerth "Rhun ap Iorwerth"), [Daisy Cooper](/wiki/Daisy_Cooper "Daisy Cooper"), [Stephen Flynn](/wiki/Stephen_Flynn "Stephen Flynn"), [Angela Rayner](/wiki/Angela_Rayner "Angela Rayner") and [Penny Mordaunt](/wiki/Penny_Mordaunt "Penny Mordaunt").[[318]](#cite_note-333) The debate included exchanges between Mordaunt and Rayner over tax, and all the attendees criticised Sunak leaving the D-Day events early; Farage called Sunak's actions "disgraceful" and said veterans had been deserted, Cooper said it was "politically shameful" and Mordaunt said Sunak's choice to leave prematurely had been "completely wrong".[[319]](#cite_note-334)[[320]](#cite_note-335) After the seven-way debate, a snap poll found that viewers considered Farage had won, followed by Rayner, but that Flynn, Denyer and Cooper scored best on doing a good job.[[321]](#cite_note-336) Another debate between these leaders took place on 13 June, with Julie Etchingham as moderator.[[322]](#cite_note-337)[[323]](#cite_note-338)

On 12 June Sky News hosted a leaders' event in [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby "Grimsby") hosted by [Beth Rigby](/wiki/Beth_Rigby "Beth Rigby"), including Starmer and Sunak, where they took questions from both Rigby and the audience.[[324]](#cite_note-339) The debate covered various topics, including the NHS, the economy, education, immigration, housing and their future plans in government. Starmer started the event by saying he was putting the country ahead of his party, bringing Labour "back into the service of working people". He went on to attack the Conservatives on tax policy, saying that "the Tories are in no position to lecture anyone about tax rises".[[325]](#cite_note-340)[[326]](#cite_note-341) 64% of those questioned by YouGov immediately following the debate said that Starmer had performed better, compared to 36% who said Sunak had performed better.[[327]](#cite_note-342)

[Channel 4 News](/wiki/Channel_4_News "Channel 4 News") hosted a debate on 18 June with all seven of the main parties focusing solely on the issues of [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") and [law and order](/wiki/Law_and_order_(politics) "Law and order (politics)").[[328]](#cite_note-343) Other BBC debates included three *[Question Time](/wiki/Question_Time_(TV_programme) "Question Time (TV programme)")* specials, two hosted by [Fiona Bruce](/wiki/Fiona_Bruce "Fiona Bruce") on 20 and 28 June, and one hosted by Bethan Rhys Roberts on 24 June. The first of the two hosted by Bruce featured four separate half-hour question and answer sessions with Sunak, Starmer, Davey and Swinney; the second of the two hosted by Bruce featured the same format with Ramsay and Farage; the programme hosted by Rhys Roberts featured the same format with ap Iorwerth. There was a BBC Cymru Wales debate on 21 June;[[329]](#cite_note-BBCDebates-344) and a debate between Sunak and Starmer hosted by Husain took place on 26 June.[[330]](#cite_note-345) There was also a BBC debate on 27 June involving the five largest Northern Irish political parties.[[329]](#cite_note-BBCDebates-344)
