### Interviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Interviews")]

In addition to the debates, the BBC and ITV broadcast programmes in which the leaders of the main parties were interviewed at length.[[348]](#cite_note-370)[[349]](#cite_note-371) [Nick Robinson](/wiki/Nick_Robinson_(journalist) "Nick Robinson (journalist)") did [BBC *Panorama*](/wiki/Panorama_(British_TV_programme) "Panorama (British TV programme)") interviews with all the leaders of the main parties. Sunak's *[Tonight](/wiki/Tonight_(1999_TV_programme) "Tonight (1999 TV programme)")* interview with [Paul Brand](/wiki/Paul_Brand_(journalist) "Paul Brand (journalist)") drew substantial coverage in the week prior to broadcast, as Sunak controversially departed the D-Day commemorations early to attend. It was later revealed that the interview slot had been chosen by Sunak and his team from a range of options offered by ITN.[[350]](#cite_note-372)

## Endorsements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Endorsements")]

Main article: [Endorsements in the 2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Endorsements_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Endorsements in the 2024 United Kingdom general election")

Newspapers, organisations, and individuals endorsed parties or individual candidates for the election.

## Candidates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Candidates")]

Main article: [Candidates in the 2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Candidates_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Candidates in the 2024 United Kingdom general election")

There were 4,515 candidates standing, which constitutes a record number, with a mean of 6.95 candidates per constituency. No seat had fewer than five people contesting it; [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak")'s [Richmond and Northallerton](/wiki/Richmond_and_Northallerton "Richmond and Northallerton") seat had the most candidates, with thirteen.[[351]](#cite_note-373)

MPs who [stood down at the election](/wiki/List_of_MPs_who_stood_down_at_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs who stood down at the 2024 United Kingdom general election") included the former prime minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"), the former cabinet ministers [Sajid Javid](/wiki/Sajid_Javid "Sajid Javid"), [Dominic Raab](/wiki/Dominic_Raab "Dominic Raab"), [Matt Hancock](/wiki/Matt_Hancock "Matt Hancock"), [Ben Wallace](/wiki/Ben_Wallace_(politician) "Ben Wallace (politician)"), [Nadhim Zahawi](/wiki/Nadhim_Zahawi "Nadhim Zahawi"), [Kwasi Kwarteng](/wiki/Kwasi_Kwarteng "Kwasi Kwarteng"), and [Michael Gove](/wiki/Michael_Gove "Michael Gove"), the long-serving Labour MPs [Harriet Harman](/wiki/Harriet_Harman "Harriet Harman") and [Margaret Beckett](/wiki/Margaret_Beckett "Margaret Beckett"), and the former Green Party leader and co-leader [Caroline Lucas](/wiki/Caroline_Lucas "Caroline Lucas"), who was the first – and until this election the only – Green Party MP.[[352]](#cite_note-374)

In March 2022, Labour abandoned [all-women shortlists](/wiki/All-women_shortlists "All-women shortlists"), citing legal advice that continuing to use them for choosing parliamentary candidates would be an unlawful practice under the [Equality Act 2010](/wiki/Equality_Act_2010 "Equality Act 2010"), since the majority of Labour MPs were now women.[[353]](#cite_note-375)

In March 2024, Reform UK announced an [electoral pact](/wiki/Reform_UK%E2%80%93TUV_alliance "Reform UK–TUV alliance") with the Northern Irish unionist party [TUV](/wiki/Traditional_Unionist_Voice "Traditional Unionist Voice").[[354]](#cite_note-376)[[355]](#cite_note-377) The TUV applied to run candidates as "TUV/Reform UK" on ballot papers, but this was rejected by the Electoral Office.[[356]](#cite_note-378) [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") unilaterally ended this deal by endorsing two competing candidates from the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") on 10 June.[[357]](#cite_note-379) Reform UK also announced a pact with the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(UK,_1990%E2%80%93present) "Social Democratic Party (UK, 1990–present)") (SDP), a minor socially conservative and economically left-wing party, in some seats.[[358]](#cite_note-380)

## Opinion polling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Opinion polling")]

Main article: [Opinion polling for the 2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Opinion polling for the 2024 United Kingdom general election")

Discussion around the campaign was focused on the prospect of a change in government, as Labour maintained significant leads in opinion polling over the Conservatives, with one in five voters voting tactically.[[361]](#cite_note-384) Projections four weeks before the vote indicated a landslide victory for Labour that surpassed the one achieved by [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") at the [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election"), while comparisons were made in the media to the [1993 Canadian federal election](/wiki/1993_Canadian_federal_election "1993 Canadian federal election") due to the prospect of a potential Conservative [wipeout](/wiki/Wipeout_(elections) "Wipeout (elections)").[[113]](#cite_note-Walker_202423-128)[[114]](#cite_note-Hunt_202423-129) A [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll conducted four weeks before the vote suggested that Labour was on course for the party's biggest election victory in history, beating Blair's 1997 landslide. The poll indicated Labour could win 422 seats, while the Conservatives were projected to win 140 seats.[[362]](#cite_note-385)

Halfway through the campaign, psephologist [John Curtice](/wiki/John_Curtice "John Curtice") summarised the polls as having shown little change in the first two weeks of the campaign but that they had then shown some clear shifts. Specifically, both the Conservatives and Labour had shown a decline of a few percentage points, leaving the gap between them unchanged, while Reform UK and the Liberal Democrats had both shown an increase, with one YouGov poll published 13 June attracting attention for showing Reform UK one point above the Conservatives.[[363]](#cite_note-386)[[364]](#cite_note-387)

### Graphical summaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Graphical summaries")]

This section is an excerpt from [Opinion polling for the 2024 United Kingdom general election § Graphical summaries](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election#Graphical_summaries "Opinion polling for the 2024 United Kingdom general election").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Opinion_polling_for_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit)]

The Conservatives led the polls for the two years following the 2019 general election, which included [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement"), the onset of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") and [vaccine rollout](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 vaccination in the United Kingdom") during the leadership of Boris Johnson. Labour took a lead following the [Partygate](/wiki/Partygate "Partygate") scandal and maintained this through the [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") and [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") premierships until the 2024 election.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Opinion_polling_for_the_next_United_Kingdom_general_election_after_2019_%28LOESS%29.svg/1280px-Opinion_polling_for_the_next_United_Kingdom_general_election_after_2019_%28LOESS%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Opinion_polling_for_the_next_United_Kingdom_general_election_after_2019_(LOESS).svg)
Opinion polls conducted for the 2024 United Kingdom general election from the 2019 general election with a local regressions (LOESS) trend line for each party.

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Results")]

Main articles: [Results of the 2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Results_of_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "Results of the 2024 United Kingdom general election"), [Candidates of the 2024 United Kingdom general election by constituency](/wiki/Candidates_of_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election_by_constituency "Candidates of the 2024 United Kingdom general election by constituency"), and [List of MPs who lost their seat in the 2024 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/List_of_MPs_who_lost_their_seat_in_the_2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs who lost their seat in the 2024 United Kingdom general election")

Voting closed at 22:00, which was followed by an exit poll. The first seat, [Houghton and Sunderland South](/wiki/Houghton_and_Sunderland_South "Houghton and Sunderland South"), was declared at 23:15 with [Bridget Phillipson](/wiki/Bridget_Phillipson "Bridget Phillipson") winning for Labour.[[401]](#cite_note-428)[[402]](#cite_note-Guardian_Results-429) [Inverness, Skye and West Ross-shire](/wiki/Inverness,_Skye_and_West_Ross-shire "Inverness, Skye and West Ross-shire") was the last seat to declare, due to multiple recounts after the election, with [Angus MacDonald](/wiki/Angus_MacDonald_(Liberal_Democrat_politician) "Angus MacDonald (Liberal Democrat politician)") winning for the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") on the afternoon of 6 July.[[403]](#cite_note-430) Notably, the 2024 election was the most unrepresentative in UK history, with Labour receiving the lowest percentage of the popular vote for a governing party in any general election since record-keeping of the popular vote began [in 1830](/wiki/1830_United_Kingdom_general_election "1830 United Kingdom general election").[[404]](#cite_note-431)[[405]](#cite_note-432)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Runners_up_in_the_2024_UK_GE.svg/250px-Runners_up_in_the_2024_UK_GE.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Runners_up_in_the_2024_UK_GE.svg)

Runners up in each constituency

The result was a landslide win for [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and a historic loss for the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"). It was the latter's worst result since formalising as a party in the early 19th century, and the largest defeat for any incarnation of the [Tories](/wiki/Tories "Tories") in purely numeric terms since [1761](/wiki/1761_British_general_election "1761 British general election") when they achieved 112 MPs, up from their all time numeric low of 106 in [1754](/wiki/1754_British_general_election "1754 British general election"), although those made up a higher percentage of seats in Parliament (18.9% in 1754 and 20.1% in 1761 as opposed to 18.6% in 2024) due to the smaller sized House of Commons in 1754–1761 of 557 MPs. The Conservatives won no seats in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") or various English counties, including [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire") (the latter historically known for having several safe Conservative seats), and they only won one seat in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England").[[410]](#cite_note-historicfirsts-449) Keir Starmer became the fourth prime minister in a two-year period.[[411]](#cite_note-450) Turnout, at 59.9%, was the second lowest since records began in [1885](/wiki/1885_United_Kingdom_general_election "1885 United Kingdom general election") with only [2001](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") being lower at 59.4%.[[412]](#cite_note-451)

The [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") made significant gains to reach their highest ever number of seats, mostly gaining Conservative seats in [Southern England](/wiki/Southern_England "Southern England"). This was also the best performance since its predecessor [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") won 158 seats, in third place, in [1923](/wiki/1923_United_Kingdom_general_election "1923 United Kingdom general election")[[413]](#cite_note-452) (itself a higher score than the second-placed Conservatives achieved in this 2024 election) and superior to Labour's [1918](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election") and [1931](/wiki/1931_United_Kingdom_general_election "1931 United Kingdom general election") performances, as second-placed official Opposition on both occasions. The [Co-operative Party](/wiki/Co-operative_Party "Co-operative Party"), which is in an electoral pact with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), and whose MPs are designated as [Labour Co-op](/wiki/Labour_Co-op "Labour Co-op"), also had its best ever result. It elected 43 MPs, surpassing its previous record of 38 seats in the [2017 election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"). These MPs are typically included in the overall Labour total. [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") had MPs elected to the Commons for the first time. Their leader, [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), was elected to Parliament on what was his eighth attempt.[[414]](#cite_note-453) The [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") also won a record number of seats.[[410]](#cite_note-historicfirsts-449) The party's two co-leaders, [Carla Denyer](/wiki/Carla_Denyer "Carla Denyer") and [Adrian Ramsay](/wiki/Adrian_Ramsay "Adrian Ramsay"), both entered Parliament for the first time.[[415]](#cite_note-454)

The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) lost around three quarters of its seats to [Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour").[[3]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk22-8) Labour returned to being the largest party in Scotland and remained so in Wales, although their vote share fell in Wales.[[416]](#cite_note-455)

Because the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") lost 3 seats, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") won the most seats in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), making it the first time an Irish nationalist party was the largest party in Parliament from Northern Ireland. The [Traditional Unionist Voice](/wiki/Traditional_Unionist_Voice "Traditional Unionist Voice") entered the Commons for the first time.[[417]](#cite_note-456) In [North Down](/wiki/North_Down_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Down (UK Parliament constituency)"), independent Unionist candidate [Alex Easton](/wiki/Alex_Easton "Alex Easton") emerged victorious over the [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland") incumbent [Stephen Farry](/wiki/Stephen_Farry "Stephen Farry"),[[418]](#cite_note-457) resulting in the election of a total of six independent MPs across the UK.

Four [independent candidates](/wiki/Independent_candidates "Independent candidates") ([Ayoub Khan](/wiki/Ayoub_Khan "Ayoub Khan"), [Adnan Hussain](/wiki/Adnan_Hussain "Adnan Hussain"), [Iqbal Mohamed](/wiki/Iqbal_Mohamed "Iqbal Mohamed"), [Shockat Adam](/wiki/Shockat_Adam "Shockat Adam")) outright defeated Labour candidates as well as one ([Claudia Webbe](/wiki/Claudia_Webbe "Claudia Webbe")) acting as a spoiler to defeat one in areas with large [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") populations; the results were suggested to be a push-back against Labour's stance on the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and the [humanitarian crisis](/wiki/Gaza_humanitarian_crisis_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Gaza humanitarian crisis (2023–present)").[[419]](#cite_note-458)[[420]](#cite_note-459) Additionally, [Wes Streeting](/wiki/Wes_Streeting "Wes Streeting") retained his [Ilford North](/wiki/Ilford_North "Ilford North") constituency by a margin of only 528 votes following a challenge by independent British-Palestinian candidate [Leanne Mohamad](/wiki/Leanne_Mohamad "Leanne Mohamad"),[[421]](#cite_note-460) while prominent Labour MP [Jess Phillips](/wiki/Jess_Phillips "Jess Phillips") retained her [Birmingham Yardley](/wiki/Birmingham_Yardley "Birmingham Yardley") constituency by a margin of 693 votes.[[422]](#cite_note-461) Labour candidate [Paul Waugh](/wiki/Paul_Waugh "Paul Waugh") won the seat of [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Rochdale (UK Parliament constituency)") from incumbent MP [George Galloway](/wiki/George_Galloway "George Galloway"), despite the constituency's sizeable Muslim population.[[423]](#cite_note-462) In [Islington North](/wiki/Islington_North "Islington North"), [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") defeated the Labour candidate with a majority of 7,247; Corbyn is a prominent activist for Palestinian solidarity.[[424]](#cite_note-463) In September 2024, Corbyn went on to form the [Independent Alliance](/wiki/Independent_Alliance_(UK) "Independent Alliance (UK)") group in parliament with four of the other five elected independent MPs.[[425]](#cite_note-IA-464)

### Proportionality concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Proportionality concerns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Proportional_results_of_the_2024_UK_General_Election.svg/250px-Proportional_results_of_the_2024_UK_General_Election.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Proportional_results_of_the_2024_UK_General_Election.svg)

Seats won in the election (outer ring) versus number of votes (inner ring)

The election was highly [disproportionate](/wiki/Disproportionality "Disproportionality"), as Labour won 63% of seats (411) with only 34% of the vote, making this the lowest vote share for a governing party [since 1830](/wiki/1830_United_Kingdom_general_election "1830 United Kingdom general election"),[[426]](#cite_note-hocl-20240924-465) while Reform won under 0.8% of seats (5) with 14.3% of the vote under the UK's [first-past-the-post voting system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting_system "First-past-the-post voting system").[[427]](#cite_note-466) The combined vote share for Labour and the Conservatives reached a record low, with smaller parties doing well. The Liberal Democrats recorded their best ever seat result (72), despite receiving only around half the votes they did in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"),[[428]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-467) and fewer votes overall than Reform, although the party's seat share was again lower than its share of the vote. As Starmer's government was elected with the lowest share of the vote of any majority party on record, journalist [Fraser Nelson](/wiki/Fraser_Nelson "Fraser Nelson") described Labour's electoral success as a "[Potemkin](/wiki/Potemkin_village "Potemkin village") landslide".[[429]](#cite_note-468) An [editorial](/wiki/Editorial "Editorial") from *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* described the result as a "crisis of electoral legitimacy" for the incoming Labour government.[[428]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-467)

The [Gallagher index](/wiki/Gallagher_index "Gallagher index") gave the election a 23.67 score, making it the least proportionate election in modern UK history according to the index, as well as the 5th least proportional result worldwide as of 2024.[[430]](#cite_note-data-469) According to political scientist [John Curtice](/wiki/John_Curtice "John Curtice"), the 2024 election was the most disproportional in British history and Labour's parliamentary majority was "heavily exaggerated" by the voting system.[[431]](#cite_note-470) Advocacy group [Make Votes Matter](/wiki/Make_Votes_Matter "Make Votes Matter") found that 58% of voters did not vote for their elected MP. Make Votes Matter spokesman Steve Gilmore, [Electoral Reform Society](/wiki/Electoral_Reform_Society "Electoral Reform Society") chief [Darren Hughes](/wiki/Darren_Hughes "Darren Hughes"), Reform UK leader Nigel Farage and the Green Party of England and Wales co-leader Adrian Ramsay were among the figures that called for electoral reform in the wake of the election. The campaigners said it was the "most disproportionate election in [British] history".[[432]](#cite_note-471)[[433]](#cite_note-472)

### Marginality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Marginality")]

The election resulted in more widespread [marginality](/wiki/Marginal_seat "Marginal seat"), with 31% of seats won on margins up to 10% compared with 22% in the 2019 election.[[434]](#cite_note-473) The average seat majority fell from 26% in 2019 to 16% in 2024.[[435]](#cite_note-474)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Prime_Minister_Rishi_Sunak_leaves_10_Downing_Street_%2853836890030%29.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_Rishi_Sunak_leaves_10_Downing_Street_%2853836890030%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Rishi_Sunak_leaves_10_Downing_Street_(53836890030).jpg)

Sunak giving his final speech as Prime Minister

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_arrives_at_Number_10_Downing_St_%2853836916571%29.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_arrives_at_Number_10_Downing_St_%2853836916571%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_arrives_at_Number_10_Downing_St_(53836916571).jpg)

Starmer giving his first speech as Prime Minister

At around 04:45 on 5 July, Sunak [conceded defeat](/wiki/Concession_(politics) "Concession (politics)") to Starmer at the declaration at Sunak's seat of [Richmond and Northallerton](/wiki/Richmond_and_Northallerton "Richmond and Northallerton"), before Labour had officially secured a majority.[[439]](#cite_note-478) In his resignation speech later that morning, Sunak apologised to Conservative voters and candidates for the party's defeat, and offered his congratulations to Starmer and expressed hope he would be successful, saying:[[440]](#cite_note-479)

> Whilst he has been my political opponent, Sir Keir Starmer will shortly become our Prime Minister. In this job, his successes will be all our successes, and I wish him and his family well. Whatever our disagreements in this campaign, he is a decent, public-spirited man, who I respect. He and his family deserve the very best of our understanding, as they make the huge transition to their new lives behind this door, and as he grapples with this most demanding of jobs in an increasingly unstable world.

Starmer succeeded Sunak as prime minister, ending 14 years of the Conservatives in government. In his first speech as prime minister, Starmer paid tribute to Sunak, saying "His achievement as the first British Asian Prime Minister of our country should not be underestimated by anyone", and recognised "the dedication and hard work he brought to his leadership" but said that the people of Britain had voted for change:[[441]](#cite_note-480)[[442]](#cite_note-481)

> From now on, you have a government unburdened by doctrine guided only by the determination to serve your interest, to defy, quietly, those who have written our country off. You have given us a clear mandate, and we will use it to deliver change. To restore service and respect to politics, end the era of noisy performance, tread more lightly on your lives and unite our country. Four nations standing together again, facing down as we have so often in our past, the challenges of an insecure world committed to a calm and patient rebuilding. So with respect and humility, I invite you all to join this government of service in the mission of national renewal. Our work is urgent, and we begin it today.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2024_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Given that Sinn Féin [members of Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_Parliament "Members of Parliament") (MPs) practise [abstentionism](/wiki/Abstentionism "Abstentionism") and do not take their seats, while the Speaker and deputies do not vote, the number of MPs needed for a majority is in practice slightly lower.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Sinn Féin won seven seats, and considering the speaker and their three deputy speakers, a practical majority requires 320 seats.
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Speaker_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Speaker_4-1) The figure does not include [Lindsay Hoyle](/wiki/Lindsay_Hoyle "Lindsay Hoyle"), the [speaker of the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)"), who was included in the Labour seat total by some media outlets. By long-standing convention, the Speaker severs all ties to their affiliated party upon being elected speaker.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-fn1_5-0)** Seat change compared to notional 2019 result under new constituency boundaries. This means Labour won 200 notional seats in 2019.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-fn2_6-0)** Seat change compared to notional 2019 result under new constituency boundaries. This means the Conservatives won 372 notional seats in 2019.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-fn3_7-0)** Seat change compared to notional 2019 result under new constituency boundaries. This means the Liberal Democrats won 8 notional seats in 2019.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** Including 43 [Labour Co-op](/wiki/Labour_Co-op "Labour Co-op") MPs
7. **[^](#cite_ref-NI_14-0)** In Northern Ireland, voter ID was already required at elections before it was introduced in the rest of the UK.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-54)** On 29 May 2024, the UK Parliamentary website stated there were 345 Conservative MPs and 205 Labour MPs. However it describes Lloyd Russell-Moyle and Mark Logan as Labour and Conservative MPs respectively at the time of dissolution, despite Logan having defected from the Conservatives and Rusell-Moyle having lost the Labour Party whip prior to dissolution.[[45]](#cite_note-52)[[46]](#cite_note-53)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-Labour_56-0)** Includes 24 MPs sponsored by the [Co-operative Party](/wiki/Co-operative_Party "Co-operative Party"), who are designated [Labour and Co-operative](/wiki/Labour_and_Co-operative "Labour and Co-operative").[[47]](#cite_note-55)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-indies_57-0)**  At the time of the dissolution of Parliament on 30 May 2024, eight of the independent MPs had been elected as Conservatives at the 2019 general election, including [Andrew Bridgen](/wiki/Andrew_Bridgen "Andrew Bridgen"), who defected to [Reclaim](/wiki/Reclaim_Party "Reclaim Party") in May 2023 but left the party in December 2023 and for the remainder of the 2019–2024 Parliament sat as an independent. The remaining nine independent MPs all came from the opposition benches.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-59)** Both of the Alba Party's MPs, [Kenny MacAskill](/wiki/Kenny_MacAskill "Kenny MacAskill") and [Neale Hanvey](/wiki/Neale_Hanvey "Neale Hanvey"), were elected for the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) before leaving to join Alba in 2021.[[48]](#cite_note-58)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-60)** Known as the Brexit Party at the 2019 election.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-voting_total_63-0)** In 2019-24, the seven members of Sinn Féin followed a policy of abstentionism. They did not swear into the house, and did not take part in its formal processes (to do so would have compelled a by-election).[[49]](#cite_note-abstain-61) The speaker and deputy speakers (at this Parliament's dissolution, three Conservative and one Labour) by convention exercise only a casting vote.[[50]](#cite_note-62)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-deputyspeakers_64-0)** Deputy speaker [Eleanor Laing](/wiki/Eleanor_Laing "Eleanor Laing") (Con., Chair of Ways and Means) was on an extended leave of absence, and [Roger Gale](/wiki/Roger_Gale "Roger Gale") (Con.) served as an additional acting deputy speaker from December 2022 until the end of the Parliament.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** The Fixed-term Parliaments Act automatically scheduled general elections for the first Thursday in May of the fifth year after the previous general election.[[52]](#cite_note-66) The previous election was held in December 2019.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-351)** Debate took place in Gilbert Scott Building's Bute Hall.
17. **[^](#cite_ref-352)** Not a debate: Sunak and Starmer separately answered questions from the studio audience.
18. **[^](#cite_ref-357)** This debate was focused solely on the issues of immigration and law and order.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-359)** Not a debate: the party leaders were each separately asked questions by the studio audience.
20. **[^](#cite_ref-361)** Not a debate: the party leaders were both separately asked questions.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-364)** Not a debate.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-366)** Not a debate: the party leaders were each separately asked questions by the studio audience.
23. **[^](#cite_ref-383)** In electoral pact with the Labour Party, all candidates are also standing for the Labour Party
